result:
## The Unbearable Lightness of Wasting Time

We are, all of us, haunted by a ghost. It is not the ghost of a person, but of a possibility. It is the phantom of the person we could have been, the life we could have lived, the art we could have made, the love we could have deepened, had we not—and here the specter points a bony, accusatory finger—*wasted so much time*.

The accusation is internal, of course. It is the bedrock of our modern anxiety. We live in an age of unprecedented access to tools of production, connection, and self-improvement. The sum total of human knowledge vibrates in our pockets. And yet, the dominant experience of the day is often one of diffuse guilt, of having frittered away the hours on the trivial, the ephemeral, the endlessly scrolling. We watch videos of other people restoring old furniture, while the chair in our garage grows another layer of dust. We read listicles about “maximizing your morning routine” as we hit snooze for the third time. We are trapped in what the anthropologist Ernest Becker might have called the “vital lie” of our era: the belief that we are optimizing ourselves, when we are often just anaesthetizing ourselves against the sheer, terrifying freedom of an unscheduled moment.

But what if we have it backwards? What if “wasting time” is not the crime, but the neglected and maligned cure?

I am not speaking of the procrastination born of fear—the frozen panic before a blank page or a difficult conversation. That is a different animal. I am speaking of the deliberate, the conscious, the *chosen* act of futility. The activity that has no measurable outcome, contributes to no skill, builds no network, burns no calories, and earns no currency. The activity whose only purpose is its own experience.

Consider the Siesta.

In large parts of the world, this is not laziness; it is a cultural institution. In the fierce heat of the afternoon, productivity is not just difficult, it is offensive to the laws of physics and biology. The world closes its shutters. Work ceases. The streets empty. And people… sleep. Or they don’t sleep. They lie in a dim room, listening to the buzz of a fly, feeling the slow turn of the earth. They are not recharging for more work. They are surrendering to the day’s natural rhythm, acknowledging that human will is not supreme. In that suspended hour, the mind is free to drift along its own backwaters, untethered from the linear demands of the clock. Solutions to problems that hours of grinding logic could not solve sometimes float to the surface in such states, not because you were seeking them, but precisely because you had stopped.

Now consider its polar opposite: The Video Game.

Specifically, the open-world, sandbox game. You are given a vast, beautiful, intricate landscape. There is a main quest—a damsel to rescue, a kingdom to save, an evil to thwart. And then there is everything else. You can spend three real-world hours meticulously picking aloe plants and crafting potions you will never use. You can climb the highest mountain just to see the pixelated sunset. You can try to herd virtual sheep into a funny shape. You are, by the metrics of the “main quest,” wasting time. You are failing to progress. The narrative is stalled. And yet, for millions, this is the precise source of joy. It is the pleasure of pure inhabitation, of existing in a world without a pressing purpose. It is a vacation from consequence. The game, in its gentle way, teaches us that play for play’s sake is a valid mode of being. It is a digital siesta.

Our horror of wasted time is a historical invention, the bastard child of the Protestant work ethic and industrial capitalism. Pre-industrial life was certainly not easy, but it was often seasonal, cyclical, and punctuated by forced idle time—long winters, nights without artificial light, periods of waiting for weather or crops. The factory whistle shattered that rhythm, replacing it with the tyranny of the punch clock. Time became a commodity. It was *spent*, *saved*, *invested*, and *wasted*. Leisure itself had to be “productive”—improving, educational, building the body or the mind. Even our relaxation became a performance.

This mentality has now reached its apotheosis in the “self-care” industry, which often seems less about care and more about the efficient maintenance of a human unit. We are told to meditate *to reduce stress and increase focus*. We are told to exercise *to boost productivity and live longer*. We are told to read *to gain knowledge and improve empathy*. The activity is always in service of a better, more effective future self. The moment itself is merely a means to an end. The ghost of potential future success approves. The ghost of the moment we are actually living in withers.

What dies in this relentless optimization is the capacity for wonder. Wonder is inefficient. Wonder stops you in your tracks. Wonder makes you stare at the intricate pattern of frost on a windowpane for ten minutes when you should be making breakfast. Wonder leads you down a Wikipedia rabbit hole about the history of pencils when you have a deadline looming. Wonder is the enemy of the checklist. And yet, wonder is the faculty that connects us most deeply to the sheer weirdness and beauty of being alive. A child is a master of wasted time. They can be captivated by the journey of an ant, the sound of a radiator, the feel of a texture. They are not building their resume. They are building their relationship with reality.

I propose a rehabilitation of two specific, maligned forms of time-wasting: **Boredom** and **Pointless Craft**.

Boredom has been pathologized. We see it as a state to be avoided at all costs, a vacuum that must be filled with stimulus. Hence, we reach for the phone in any idle second—in line, in a waiting room, in the passenger seat. But boredom is not empty. It is a fertile void. It is the itchy, restless feeling that arises when the external world fails to provide easy entertainment. In that discomfort, the internal world is forced to stir. It is from boredom that daydreams are born, that stories are invented, that the mind turns inward to examine its own contents. The poet Joseph Brodsky famously told graduates, “When you find yourself bored, bear it. It is a gateway.” A gateway to what? To yourself. To the raw, un-curated contents of your own consciousness, before it has been shaped into tweets or presentations or polite conversation. To kill boredom the instant it arises is to forever remain a tourist in your own mind, only visiting the pre-packaged attractions.

Pointless Craft is my term for making or doing something with no practical goal, no audience, and no possibility of improvement. It is the adult equivalent of a child’s mud pie. Knitting a scarf that is too lumpy and too long for anyone to wear, then unravelling it. Building an elaborate model ship inside a glass bottle, then putting it in the attic. Writing a poem in a language you are inventing as you go. Gardening with a focus on arranging colors in a way that pleases only you, with no regard for harvest. The key is the divorce from outcome. You are not “learning to knit.” You are engaging in the rhythmic, tactile ritual of knotting yarn. The value is in the *doing*, the state of flow it induces, the quiet companionship of your own hands at work on a useless task. It is a meditation with a material witness. In a world obsessed with deliverables, pointless craft is a gentle rebellion. It declares that some activities are worthy simply because they make the present moment more rich and textured.

This is not an argument for sloth, or for abandoning responsibility. Bills must be paid. Commitments must be honored. The people who depend on us deserve our focused effort. This is an argument for balance, for carving out—or more accurately, *defending*—small, sacred spaces of intentional futility.

Because these spaces are where we encounter the self that exists outside of its utility. We are not just workers, consumers, parents, or partners. We are also perceivers, wonderers, dabblers, and dreamers. That part of us needs nourishment too, and its food is often what the productive world calls “waste.”

Think of the long, meandering walk with no destination. You are not “getting your steps in.” You are following a curious alleyway. You are stopping to watch a argument between two sparrows. You are smelling the incongruous smell of laundry detergent from a basement vent. You are, for a little while, a sensory being in a sensory world, not a brain on legs hurrying to the next task. You are collecting impressions, not achievements.

Or consider the lost art of the visit. Not the scheduled playdate or the networking coffee, but the dropping by. The sitting on a porch or a kitchen stool with a friend or a neighbor, talking about nothing in particular. The conversation loops, digresses, goes silent, picks up again. It might last ten minutes or three hours. No agenda is fulfilled. No problem is solved. But a connection is woven, stitch by pointless stitch, in the shared space of unmeasured time.

Our fear of wasting time is, at its root, a fear of death. It is the nervous calculus that whispers: *You have a finite number of heartbeats. Are you using them correctly?* The ghost of our potential is the *memento mori* of the ambitious. But this calculus is flawed. It assumes that only certain types of moments “count.” It privileges the future over the present. It turns life into a race to accumulate validated experiences before the clock runs out.

But what if the moments of so-called waste are the very ones that give life its weight, its flavor, its subjective meaning? The memory of a perfect, idle afternoon fishing with a grandparent (and catching nothing) may outlast the memory of a promotion. The recollection of laughing uncontrollably with a friend over a stupid, drawn-out joke carries more emotional mass than the memory of a efficient, productive workday.

Perhaps we need a new metaphor. Time is not a currency to be spent, nor a limited resource to be hoarded. It is the medium in which we swim. It is the water, not the fish. We cannot “save” it; we can only *inhabit* it, with varying degrees of depth and attention. To be always rushing through it, towards some future point of accomplishment, is to live like a man so focused on crossing the ocean that he never feels the water on his skin, never sees the play of light on the waves, never dives below the surface to see the strange, luminous creatures there.

The Japanese have a concept, ***ma***. It is often translated as “negative space” or “pause.” It is the purposeful interval, the gap, the silence between notes that makes the music. It is not emptiness, but a charged emptiness, full of potential and awareness. A painting without *ma* is cluttered and suffocating. A life without *ma* is a relentless, noisy cacophony of doing. Our wasted time, our bored moments, our pointless crafts—these are the *ma* of a human life. They are the spaces where meaning breathes.

So, I am beginning to experiment. I am trying to waste time beautifully, intentionally, and without apology.

I will sit in a chair and watch a beam of sunlight travel across the floor, tracking the dust motes dancing in it. I will not take a photo. I will not turn it into a metaphor for a poem. I will just watch.

I will start a jigsaw puzzle of a monotonous, difficult scene—a sky full of clouds—knowing it may take weeks, that no one will care when it’s done, and that I will just break it up and put it back in the box.

I will call a friend and say, “Tell me about your dream last night,” and listen to the whole, confusing, nonsensical narrative.

I will bake a complicated cake on a Tuesday for no reason, and eat a slice alone, standing over the kitchen counter, savoring the pure, ephemeral sugar of it.

In these acts, I am not building a better future me. I am feeding the present me. I am placating a different ghost—not the ghost of my potential, but the ghost of my actual, lived experience. I am telling it: *This moment, too, is valid. This idleness is not a debt to your future, but a dividend paid to your now.*

The world will not stop its frantic drumbeat. The emails will still ping. The to-do lists will still regenerate. The ghost of potential will still whisper from the shadows. But in the small, defiant cathedrals of wasted time that we build for ourselves, we might just hear something else: the quiet, persistent, and endlessly interesting sound of our own being, humming along for no reason at all, and find that it is, in fact, enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
